subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Interest Groups vs. Social Movements and Participatory Democracy
stimulus: Both interest groups and social movements provide avenues for political participation. Develop an argument as to whether interest groups or social movements better reflect the participatory model of democracy.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Federalist No. 10, The First Amendment of the United States Constitution, "Letter from a Birmingham Jail".)
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about both providing avenues for participation do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Interest groups better reflect the participatory model because they bring together people with common goals.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Interest groups better reflect the participatory model of democracy because they are an established part of the policy making process.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Social movements better reflect the participatory model, and they are inclusive.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Social movements better reflect the participatory model of democracy since people can directly and immediately create the change they seek.”

wrong_examples: “Interest groups better reflect the participatory model of democracy. Do not respond to the prompt
“Social movements are better at pressuring the government to change laws.”
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 3 points, must have earned Row A, and one piece of evidence must come from Federalist 10, the First Amendment, or Letter from Birmingham Jail.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

required_elements: No accurate or relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Must provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: Evidence must be relevant but need not directly support a specific claim to earn 1 point

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence.

required_elements: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. OR
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. OR

required_elements: Must provide two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Must provide two pieces of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: To earn 2 points, the response must have earned Row A (claim/thesis)

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, one from a listed foundational document.

required_elements: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific
alternatives.correct_example: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific

required_elements: “Social movements give power to the people.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt
alternatives.correct_example: “Social movements give power to the people.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt

required_elements: “Letter from a Birmingham Jail was a statement in a local newspaper.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:
alternatives.correct_example: “Letter from a Birmingham Jail was a statement in a local newspaper.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:

required_elements: "Political parties organize voters based on their ideological interests.”
alternatives.correct_example: "Political parties organize voters based on their ideological interests.”

required_elements: “Social media has made it easier for groups of people to organize.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Social media has made it easier for groups of people to organize.”

required_elements: “Federalist No. 10 discussed the impact that factions can have on a republic.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Federalist No. 10 discussed the impact that factions can have on a republic.”

required_elements: “The First Amendment allows people to petition the government for change.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The First Amendment allows people to petition the government for change.”

required_elements: “Letter from a Birmingham Jail advocates for social movements, like the Civil Rights Movement.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):
alternatives.correct_example: “Letter from a Birmingham Jail advocates for social movements, like the Civil Rights Movement.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):

required_elements: “Political efficacy is the feeling that you can influence what government does. Interest groups give people a wide variety of ways to influence governmental actions.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Political efficacy is the feeling that you can influence what government does. Interest groups give people a wide variety of ways to influence governmental actions.”

required_elements: “Representatives in Congress are concerned about re-election, which makes them sensitive to activists and social movements in the places they represent.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Representatives in Congress are concerned about re-election, which makes them sensitive to activists and social movements in the places they represent.”

required_elements: “In Federalist No. 10, the Federalists advocated for a large republic because they were concerned about factions, their word for interest groups, and how factions would have too much power in a small republic or a direct democracy.”
alternatives.correct_example: “In Federalist No. 10, the Federalists advocated for a large republic because they were concerned about factions, their word for interest groups, and how factions would have too much power in a small republic or a direct democracy.”

required_elements: “The First Amendment protects the rights of assembly and free speech, which means that people can express their political views easily.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The First Amendment protects the rights of assembly and free speech, which means that people can express their political views easily.”

required_elements: “In Letter from a Birmingham Jail, Martin Luther King suggests that people shouldn’t wait for the courts to change unjust laws and argues that people should protest instead.”
alternatives.correct_example: “In Letter from a Birmingham Jail, Martin Luther King suggests that people shouldn’t wait for the courts to change unjust laws and argues that people should protest instead.”

common_traps: One piece must be from a foundational document listed in the prompt

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have provided at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence. Must explain the connection between evidence and the argument.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: "Interest groups are made up of elites, lobbyists, and concerned citizens. By bringing together a wide range of voices, interest groups reflect the participatory model of democracy because interest groups desire to satisfy the needs of their members.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Through the right of protest, which is protected by the First Amendment, social movements can get the attention of policymakers, which directly connects the people to the government and is closer to the participatory model of democracy.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “It might be true that social movements include more people, but this is only true in times of crisis or injustice. On the other hand, interest groups are always working to increase participation.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Some people might argue that social movements better reflect the participatory model of democracy because they include large groups of people. However, social movements do not always target wide segments of the population and some deliberately limit their membership to certain groups of people.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Interest groups aren’t as inclusive as one might think, since many require members to have resources. Social movements offer participation that requires little from people except their time and their voices.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Some people might argue that interest groups better represent the participatory model because they have more direct access to members of Congress. However, even with better access, interest groups only benefit a certain segment of the population, while social movements have the potential to include a much broader range of people.”

wrong_examples: “Social movements better reflect the participatory model of democracy.” Describe an alternate perspective but do not rebut or refute that perspective
“Some people might argue that social movements better reflect the participatory model because they include people who are typically outside the system.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
